title: 第 22 節
en: There are some unmistakable lines of agreement running through all these stories. Herakles and Iphikles have the same mother, but different fathers, one divine (Zeus), the other human (Amphitryon) ; so have Kastor and Polydeukes (Zeus and Tyndareus) ; and so have Dardanos and Jasion (Zeus and Korytos), and, to a certain extent, Pelias and Neleus ; Poseidon (Enipeus and Kretheus) and Amphion and Zethos (Zeus and Epopeus). The children are considered dangerous to their parents or grandparents, and are therefore either exposed or persecuted. Sometimes, in order to make them innocuous, they are sent out on desperate errands, such as to fetch the golden fleece, or to perform the twelve labours. Some of them, such as Kastor and Polydeukes, Amphion and Zethos, are actually called AuocrKovpoL, and white-horsed, \evKOTro)XoL, \evKL7nroL.

It would be useless to attempt to trace in the stories of these couples more than a very vague Vl] GREEK AND ROMAN TWINS. 579 memory of their original character, or to discover any solar elements in them beyond the twinhood of day and night, their descent from the sky or the sun, their relationship with the beautiful dawn, and their supposed dangerous character arising from the necessary destruction of the preceding day or season or year by each succeeding one. But on the other hand we can hardly believe that this fundamental theme — Rv. VIII, 29, 8, Dva £arata/i ekaya saha, ' The two move along together with the one ' — which appears in all its natural simplicity in the Veda, should occur again and again in Greek and other Aryan myths, unless there was something more in it than mere accident, or unless we believe that our common human nature is really sufficient to account for all such coincidences. Of course our common human nature, and likewise the common physical nature by which we are surrounded, have something to do with all mythology, but whereas if we had to deal with Zulus or Mincoupies we should probably have to be satisfied with this appeal to our common humanity, it seems to me that we have here a little more. We have something that has grown and become named and fixed ; we have something in a certain sense historical, and yet so prehistoric that it must have existed before the Aryan Separation, and have been carried off in its undeveloped stage by the different branches of the Aryan family.
Other Names of the Asvins.

And this is to a certain extent confirmed by the names also of some of these mythological characters. Those names, if they stood alone, might perhaps be put aside as uncertain or as the result of mere 580 0THEK NAMES OF THE ASVINS. [chap.
accident, but as additional fulcra they are of real importance.

The Asvins, as we saw, were called Divo napata, grandchildren of Dyaus, while their mother bore the name of Duhita DivaA, the daughter of Dyaus. In Greece, not only the sons of Leda, but the sons of Antiope also, were called AuoaKovpot, i. e. the sons of Zeus. The Asvins have brilliant or white horses (suka,jah), the sun itself is called the white horse, and the Dioskouroi also are supposed to ride on white horses (XcvkottcoXol, XtvKnnroi). And be it remembered that this epithet XevKoiroiXos is in Greek an epithet of Hemera also, or the daybreak.

Dasra is often used as the name of one or both of the Asvins. Their mother who, as we saw, was called YamasM, mother of the twins, was in later times called DasrasM also, the mother of the Dasras. Sayana explains dasra by destroyer of enemies, Roth by wonder-working. It may possibly be connected with das in the sense of hunting (cf. abhidas), which may have been further developed in SrjpLdofxai, to wrestle, and Srjpis, fight (cf. vas, vasar, vasra, eap, rjp), unless we connect 877/315 with Sepco, remembering that one of the Dioskouroi, Polydeukes, is always conceived as ttvktt)^, the pugilist.

Nasatya. Nasatya also is used whether of one or of both the Asvins. They are called Nasatyau in the dual, according to a very common Vedic custom which sanctions Mitra for Mitra-Vanmau, Nakta and Ushasa for Naktoshasa, Dyava and PWthivi for Dyava-PWthivi. But Nasatya occurs in the singular also, Rv. IV, 3, 6, nasatyaya pari^mane, and we have the compound Indra-Nasatya, Rv. VIII, 26, 8. What Vi] NASATYA. 581 is more perplexing is the use in Latin of Castores in the plural, meant for the dual, for Castor and Pollux (Plin. H. N. 10, 43), and according to some, even of Polluces, as comprehending both the Dioskouroi. How did the Romans evolve this use which is not sanctioned in Greek, and how did they change Polydeukes into Pollux ? It is a very useful lesson on the changeableness of mythological names, but it is difficult to explain the exact process. In Greek Dioskouroi never occurs in the singular except in grammars.

Nasatya is a troublesome name. It is explained by Sayawa as na-asatya, not untruthful, by others as na-satya, very truthful, nay even as nasatya, nasutus (Bergaigne). I doubt whether any one of these explanations has proved acceptable. My own derivation from nas is difficult, but not impossible. The root nas, meaning to come towards something, has, particularly in Greek, taken the meaning of returning. This is shown in voctto<;, return, from veofxau (Goth, nisan). An important portion of Greek mythology consisted, as is well known, in the NoVtoi or the returns of the Greek heroes. The Odyssey was in fact the Nostos of Odysseus. The heroes who return or who are saved were called vo<T7i\Loi, and in their first beginning, long before the rise of epic mythology, these terms seem to point back to the return of all the heavenly bodies, whether at the end of the night, or at the end of the winter. The Asvins are most prominently the ever-returning heroes, and nasa-tya like vocrri-juo9 would therefore have been by no means an inappropriate term for one of them, if we derived it from nasa, return, and the local suffix tya. This 582 NASATYA. [CHAP.

is no more than a guess which would carry little weight by itself, but may claim consideration as throwing light on the most distant background of Aryan mythology, and as indicating once more the physical origin of this whole cluster of myths.

On some points in the character of the Asvins the language of the Veda allows of no doubt. If it is said that Surya, the daughter of Surya, was given to the Asvins to be their wife, or that they won her in a race against all the gods, no one would doubt that Surya, sometimes called the daughter of Surya, was the sun in her female character, or the morning sun, the dawn, together with whom the Asvins drive along on their chariot which is said to strike the ends of the sky (VII, 69, 3). Sometimes they are actually called Siiryavasu, brilliant with Surya (VII, 68, 3), sometimes, as we saw, Surya is said to have chosen them for herself (VII, 69, 4). The Asvins are the sons of Vivas vat, and they are supposed, in their independent character, to dwell with him, I, 46, 1 3, vavasana Vivasvati 1. Anyhow, if any gods in heaven are solar, the Asvins are so, as representatives of day and night, starting every morning on their journey, and returning the next morning. They are in consequence invoked and worshipped both in the early morning and in the evening.

1 There is a special development in the meaning of Vivasvat which made him a priest on earth, just as among the gods Vivasvat, like Agni, performs the office of the priest. We must remember that the sacrifice on earth is often an imitation of phenomena in the sky and vice versa. In the plural the Vivasvats are a priestly family like the Angiras, the Bhrigus, &c.
Vl] ASVINS AS TEMPORAL GODS. 583 Asvins as Temporal Gods.

That everything belonging to the Asvins is golden or white would be quite consonant with their solar character, but we must not restrict their dramatic sphere to that of the sun only. They are from the beginning temporal rather than local gods, representing all that happens in the daily struggle between night and day. This temporal character of theirs accounts for their manifold and various manifestations1, and the numerous acts ascribed to them. There are few things that cannot be considered as the acts of Day and Night. Hence in the morning the Asvins may be said to bring light or to deliver the light from the fangs of the night. They also may be praised for delivering the world from darkness, and, one may add, from all the miseries which Hesiod describes 2 as the brood of the night.

During the day they may be asked to assist both the labourers and the warriors, and at the end of the day to grant victory, peace, and rest. There are few things therefore from birth to death for which different poets may not have invoked or praised the Asvins, and hence the many legends alluded to in the hymns of the Eig-veda, and taken as generally known, in which the Asvins appear as benefactors or deliverers.
Achievements of the Asvins. The persons whom they delivered may in some 1 Kv. I, 117, 9, puru varpamsi Asvina dadhana.
2 Theogony, w. 211-232.

cases have been real persons of whom of course we can know nothing. In other cases, however, those who were delivered by them were clearly, like their deliverers, mythological characters 1. The stories are very much alike, and this by itself would indicate their mythological character. There is great similarity in the misfortunes that befall their favourites, and in the manner in which they were delivered by the Asvins. The stories themselves are often so uninteresting that, unless they had rested on a mythological foundation, they would hardly have been recorded at all. Thus we read that a quail, Vartika, had been swallowed by a wolf, but was delivered by the Asvins from his jaws. Even if we took Vartika as the name of a real woman, her being saved from the jaws of a wolf would hardly have been of sufficient importance to inspire various Vedic poets, nor is it clear how the Asvins could have saved her from being swallowed, unless, with the Aitihasikas, we took them for two ancient kings.
Vartika.

Vartika 2 in Sanskrit means quail 3, and corresponds to Greek oprvi;. The etymological meaning is the returning bird, like Vertumnus, the returning or revolving year. If then the Asvins had rescued a real quail or a real woman from the jaws of a wolf, 1 See Myriantheus, Die Acvins oder Arischen Dioskuren, 2 Science of Language, ii, p. 626.
8 In Greek xeAiScoi/, the swallow, signifies the return of the year, see Bergk, p. IO34, rj^ff, rjhdt xeXtScoj', KaXas copas ayovaa, koXovs eviavTovs.

Vi] VARTIKA. 585 would that have been an event to be immortalised in Vedic mythology ? Surely we must look somewhere else to account for our Vartika myth. Vartika, the returning, might well have represented the return, either of the day or of the spring. If she represented the return of the day, i. e. the dawn, she was indeed rescued by the Asvins from the jaws of the night ; if she represented the return of the year, she might have been the spring rescued from the prison of the winter. It should also be remembered that Herakles, clearly a solar hero, was once revived by an Ortyx (Gerhard, Griech. Myth., 355, 2). We have seen how these two ideas, the matutinal and the vernal, are constantly running together in ancient mythology1. If then we find that Leto was delivered of her two children, Apollon and Artemis, in the island of Ortygia, the quail-island, we can hardly look upon this as an accidental coincidence, but have to recognise here too a very slightly altered repetition of the old story that the solar god was delivered at the return of the morning, or at the beginning of the spring. We can then understand why Apollon was called Lykoktonos, the killer of the wolf, and why his sister Artemis was called Ortygia. That island, Ortygia, which was originally a mythological postulate rather than a geographical reality, is best known by the name of Delos, and this again means the bright island or the morning island, that is the Eastern sky. The complete legend of Leto giving 1 Sonne, K. Z., x, p. 121. In Germany the stork was looked upon as the ('lyye'hos i'apos, and there is a modern epigram by Olearius : ' Ver laetum rediit, rediitque ciconia grata.' Cf. Grimm, Mythologie, p. 723.

birth to Apollon and Artemis was afterwards variously elaborated by poets and story-tellers, but the original elements of the legend need not have been more than a few proverbial sayings such as, ' The wolf has swallowed the quail,' i. e. it is night ; ' The quail has been delivered from the jaws of the wolf,' i. e. it is morning. ' The night has brought forth her twins,' i. e. a new day (day and night) has begun. Such popular sayings are all that we have a right to expect in the as yet indifferentiated state of Aryan mythology ; still if the occurrence of two such words as vartika in Sanskrit, and oprvt; ( = *vartuka) in Greek, proves that the quail was known to the Aryas before their separation, the occurrence of Vartika as saved by the Asvins from the mouth of the wolf, and the birth of Ortygia (Artemis) and of Apollon Lykoktonos in the island of Ortygia, prove that the first germs of the much deprecated Solar Myth had begun to break forth when the ancestors of Greeks and Hindus were still living in close contact together. It is easier, no doubt, to say that the quail may have been a totem or a fetish, it is quite possible also that the Aryas never ate quails, in which case we should be told that we need not look further or trouble ourselves with doubtful etymologies.

I think on the contrary that the story of the Vartika ought to be a lesson to us, and that we should learn in how many fragments an old myth may have been shattered before it reaches us. There seems at first little reason why the story of a quail being rescued from the jaws of a wolf should have had anything in common with the story of Apollon and Artemis being born in the island Vi] VARTIKA. 587 of Delos. But when we see that Artemis is called Ortygia, and that the same name is given to the island of Delos, while in the Veda the person delivered from the wolf is called Vartika, we begin to feel that there must be some reason for it, and that if we but admit that in the most distant Aryan past, Vartika meant both quail and dawn, we can understand how the old story of the delivery of the ever returning dawn from the darkness of the night or the jaws of the wolf might have assumed these different disguises in the course of centuries, nay, of thousands of years that passed between the first origin of these early sayings and the days of the Vedic iftshis or the Greek rhapsodes. Anyhow, we may learn the lesson that if in this case the beings delivered by the Asvins from the wolf were the dawn and the rising sun, other beings also, delivered by the Asvins, may have had a similar origin, and that if the Vartika represented the returning light, the wolf, killed by the Lykoktonos, could hardly have been meant for anything but the retreating darkness.
iTyavtlna.

We read in the Vedic hymns that the Asvins restored Zyavana to youth, delivered him of his decrepit body, and made him acceptable once more to his wife. We can see in this case how early these old stories were amplified and diversified, for while the hymns presuppose nothing more, we read in the jSatapatha-bnUimawa IV, 1,5 a much fuller account. We are told there that the wife of /{yavana was called Sukanya (good girl), and that she was given to the old and decrepit Rishi Jiyavana l 1 .Kyavana instead of ifyavana is the later form. VOL. II. M 588 XYAVANA. [chap.

by king *Saryata, to make up for an insult offered him by the king's people. Then the Asvins came and tried to tempt her away, but she would not leave her old husband, and even derided the Asvins. When the Asvins wished to know why she derided them, she promised to tell them if they would make her husband young again. Upon this the Asvins made Zyavana step into a pool, promising that he should come out of it at whatever age he should desire. When this had been done, ilfyavana told the Asvins that they were derided because they had formerly been excluded by the gods from a solemn sacrifice. This story may have existed when the hymn was composed, for it contains some mythological elements known in other Aryan mythologies also. There is a spring produced by Hades near the place where the earth opened to receive him and Persephone, and where Herakles is supposed to have founded a great annual festival. This spring is called Kyane. The pool, called saisava, youth, seems the type of the German Jungbrunnen, O. H. G. quickprunno, Grimm, D. M., p. 554, while Sukanya, as the wife of the old man, will remind many of the story of Eos and Tithonos1. As to the name of iTyavana, afterwards Jfyavana, it is derived from kyu, and &yu has been identified with crevco, as expressing any violent movement, not only that of driving or pushing, but also that of falling down, e. g. tara ambaraA: Ayavante, ' stars fall from the sky, or set' (Ram. V, 13, 31), or svargaA; Ayavate lokat, ' he falls from the Svarga 1 Myriantheus, 1. c, p. 94. His identification of Sukanya with Medeia seems very doubtful.

Vi] ^YAVANA. 589 world.' It may therefore have been used for the set or the setting sun, of which it might well be said that it had fallen into the fiery or dark abyss of the West, the very abyss from whence the Asvins were born, Rv. Ill, 39, 3, tapushaA budhne a ita, and that it rose again rejuvenated by the Asvins in the morning. The idea also that the Asvins removed ilyavana's body like an old cloak would find an explanation in the clouds surrounding and hiding the sun at its setting l.
Atri.

Much the same story is told of Atri. He is supposed to have been thrown into a fiery pit, sometimes called ribisa 2, an oven, another metaphor, it would seem, for a flaming sunset. The Asvins protected him from the heat and from the darkness, and restored him also to youth and strength. In this case the name of Atri is less clear than that of iiyavana, yet it may have been connected with Avestic atar, traces of which are preserved in Lat. atrium, and in Sk. athar-van, fire-priest. Others explain Atri as ad-tH, devourer, a common name for enemies of the gods (O. N. jotunn) 3. In cases like this we can do no more than suggest; we 1 I find the same explanation in V. Henry's Mythes Naturalistes, p. 12. The same scholar derives the name of Sisyphus also from the root ftyu, in the sense of pushing, and I think he is right.
zh: 這些故事中有一些無可置疑的一致線索貫穿其中。赫拉克勒斯（Herakles）和伊菲克勒斯（Iphikles）有同一位母親，但父親不同，一位是神祇（宙斯），另一位是人類（安菲特律翁）；卡斯托爾和波呂杜克斯（Kastor and Polydeukes）也是如此（宙斯和廷達瑞斯）；達爾達諾斯和雅西翁（Dardanos and Jasion）也是如此（宙斯和科律托斯）；在某種程度上，珀利亞斯和涅琉斯（Pelias and Neleus）也是如此；波塞冬（通過埃尼珀烏斯和克瑞透斯）（Poseidon, Enipeus and Kretheus）以及安菲翁和澤托斯（宙斯和埃波佩烏斯）（Amphion and Zethos, Zeus and Epopeus）。這些孩子被認為對其雙親或祖父母構成危險，因此要麼被遺棄，要麼遭受迫害。有時為了使他們變得無害，會派遣他們執行絕望的任務，譬如去取得金羊毛，或完成十二項勞役。其中有些人，例如卡斯托爾和波呂杜克斯、安菲翁和澤托斯，實際上被稱為「馬夫」（AuocrKovpoL）和「白馬的」（λευκοτπόχλοι, λευκίππoι）。

要想在這些對偶的故事中追溯他們最初特性的遠古記憶，或在其中發現任何太陽元素，除了晝夜的雙生性質、他們自天空或太陽的下降、他們與美麗黎明的關係，以及由於每個相繼的晝、季節或年份必然摧毀先前者而產生的所謂危險特性之外，恐怕都將是徒勞無功。然而另一方面，我們幾乎難以相信這一基本主題——梵文經文《梨俱吠陀》第八卷第二十九篇第八節中的「Dva śaratah ekaya saha」，意為「兩者與其一起行進」——在吠陀中以其自然樸素的形式出現，卻又一次又一次地在希臘及其他雅利安神話中重現，除非其中蘊含著超乎純粹巧合的內涵，或者除非我們相信人類共同的本性確實足以解釋所有這類巧合。當然，人類的共同本性，以及同樣包圍著我們的共同物質本性，對所有神話都有其作用，但倘若我們不得不涉及祖魯人或美因庫皮人，我們或許就必須滿足於這種訴諸人類共同性的說法。然而在此情況下，我看來我們擁有更多的東西。我們有某種已然成長、獲得命名並固定下來的東西；在某個意義上，我們有某種歷史的、然而又是如此遠古的東西，它必然存在於雅利安分化之前，並在其未發展的階段被雅利安族族所分化出的不同分支所帶離。
阿濕溫神的其他名稱
而這一點在某些神話人物的名字上也得到了某種程度的證實。這些名字，如果單獨看待，也許可以被視為不確定的，或僅僅是推測的結果。
意外之事，但作為額外的支點，它們具有真正的重要性。

阿斯溫神（Asvins）如我們所見，被稱為特尤斯之孫（Divo napata），而其母親則名為「特尤斯之女」（Duhita Diva）。在希臘，不僅勒達（Leda）之子，而且安提俄珀（Antiope）之子也被稱為宙斯之子（Αὐοακούροι），即「宙斯的兒子」。阿斯溫神擁有光燦或白色的馬匹（suka,jah），太陽本身也被稱為白馬，迪奧斯庫里人（Dioskouroi）也據稱騎乘白馬（Λευκοπώλοι、Λευκίππων）。值得注意的是，這個希臘上詞「Λευκοπώλος」（白馬騎士）在希臘語中也是赫美拉（Hemera）或曙光的修飾詞。

Dasra 常被用作雙馬童（Asvins）之一或兩者的名稱。如我們所見，他們的母親被稱為 Yamas（雙胞胎之母），在後世也被稱為 Dasras（Dasras 之母）。沙亞那（Sayana）將 dasra 解釋為「敵人的摧毀者」，羅特（Roth）則解釋為「奇蹟的製造者」。它可能與 das 有關，其意義為狩獵（參見 abhidas），這一含義可能在 Srjpλδοϕαι（摔跤）和 Srjpis（搏鬥）中得到進一步發展（參見 vas、vasar、vasra、eap、rjp），除非我們將其與 Sepco 相連繫——須記得宙斯的兒子（Dioskouroi）之一波呂得克斯（Polydeukes）始終被認為是 παγκρατής（全能搏擊者）。

**Nasatya.** Nasatya 亦可用於指稱一位或兩位阿斯溫神（Asvins）。按照梨俱吠陀中極為常見的習俗，他們以雙數形式稱為 Nasatyau；此習俗允許以 Mitra 代 Mitra-Varṇau、以 Nakta 和 Ushasa 代 Naktoshasa、以 Dyava 和 Pṛthivi 代 Dyava-Pṛthivi。然而 Nasatya 亦出現於單數形式，見《梨俱吠陀》第四卷第三讚歌第六詩節（Rv. IV, 3, 6），nasatyaya parīmane；我們亦有複合詞 Indra-Nasatya，《梨俱吠陀》第八卷第二十六讚歌第八詩節（Rv. VIII, 26, 8）。更令人困惑的是，拉丁文中使用 Castores（複數形式）本為雙數，指卡斯托與波呂克斯（Castor and Pollux），見普林尼《博物志》第十卷第四十三章（Plin. H. N. 10, 43）；據某些學者所言，甚至使用 Polluces，以涵蓋兩位迪奧斯庫里（Dioskouroi）二神。羅馬人如何演化出這種在希臘語中並無根據的用法，又如何將 Polydeukes 變成 Pollux？這是一堂關於神話名稱易變性的極為有益的課程，然而要解釋其確切的演變過程殊為困難。在希臘語中，Dioskouroi 從不出現於單數形式，除非在語法書中。

納薩特亞（Nasatya）是個麻煩的名字。薩亞瓦（Sayawa）將其解釋為那－阿薩特亞（na-asatya），意為「非虛偽」；他人則解釋為那－薩特亞（na-satya），意為「極其真實」；甚至還有人（如貝爾蓋涅〔Bergaigne〕）將其解釋為納薩特亞、納蘇圖斯（nasatya, nasutus）。我懷疑這些解釋中是否有任何一種已被證明為可接受。我自己從詞根「納斯」（nas）推導出的結論雖有難度，但並非不可能。詞根「納斯」意為「朝向某物而來」，特別是在希臘語中，已取得「返回」之義。這可見諸「返回」（νόστος）一詞，源自「我返回」（νέομαι）（哥德語 nisan）。眾所周知，希臘神話的重要部分正是「返回」（Νόστοι）或希臘英雄的回歸。《奧德賽》事實上就是奧德修斯的《返鄉記》（Nostos）。返回或被拯救的英雄被稱為「返回者」（νόστιμοι），而在其最初的起源、遠在史詩神話興起之前，這些用語似乎指向所有天體的返回——無論是在夜晚結束時，抑或在冬季結束時。雙馬童神（Asvins）最突出地體現了永遠返回的英雄，因此納薩特亞（nasa-tya）若如「返回者」（νόστιμος）那樣，從「納薩」（nasa，返回）和位置後綴「特亞」（tya）推導而來，對於他們中的一位而言，當不失為恰當的名號。
這不過是一個猜測，單獨而言份量不足，但作為闡明雅利安神話最久遠背景的嘗試，以及再次指示這整個神話叢集之物理起源的努力，則足堪考慮。

關於阿濕毘尼神（Asvins）性格的某些方面，《吠陀》的語言毫無疑義。若說蘇利耶（Surya），即蘇利耶之女，被賜予阿濕毘尼神作為他們的妻子，或者他們在與眾神的競賽中贏得她，則無人會懷疑蘇利耶，有時被稱為蘇利耶之女，乃是女性化的太陽，或晨光中的太陽、晨曦，阿濕毘尼神與之共乘其戰車，據說該戰車擊及天空的四極（VII, 69, 3）。有時他們實際上被稱為「蘇利耶瓦蘇」（Siiryavasu），以蘇利耶之光華閃耀（VII, 68, 3）；有時如我們所見，蘇利耶被說為自己選擇了他們（VII, 69, 4）。阿濕毘尼神乃是毘瓦斯瓦特（Vivasvat）之子，在他們獨立的性格上，據信與他共住（I, 46, 1‧3，vavasana Vivasvati）。無論如何，若說天上有任何提婆是太陽性的，則阿濕毘尼神就是，蓋彼等乃日夜的代表，每日晨間開始其旅程，次日晨間歸返。因此，他們在早晨與傍晚時分都被祈禱與敬拜。
維瓦斯瓦特（Vivasvat）的含義經歷了特殊的發展，使其成為地上的祭司，正如在諸神之中，維瓦斯瓦特如同阿格尼（Agni）一樣執行祭司的職能。我們必須記住，地上的祭祀往往是天空現象的模仿，反之亦然。複數形式的維瓦斯瓦特們是祭司家族，如同安吉拉斯人（Angiras）、布里古人（Bhrigus）等。
**馬神為時間之神。**

屬於雙馬神的一切事物都是金色或白色，這確實與其太陽特性相符；然而我們不應將其戲劇性的領域限制於太陽之中。雙馬神自始即為時間性而非地方性的提婆，代表著晝夜日常鬥爭中所發生的一切。正是這種時間性的特質，說明了他們眾多而各異的顯現1，以及歸屬於他們的眾多行為。幾乎沒有什麼事物不能被視為白晝與黑夜的作為。因此，在清晨時，雙馬神可被稱為帶來光明者，或從黑夜之牙中解放光明者。他們也可因解救世界脫離黑暗而受讚頌，並且可補充一句，他們從赫西俄德2所描述為黑夜之子孫的一切苦難中解救世界。
白晝時，他們可能被要求協助勞動者和戰士，而當日暮時則被祈求賜予勝利、和平與安息。因此，從出生至死亡，幾乎沒有什麼事物是不同的詩人未曾為雙馬神（Asvins）祈禱或讚美過的，因此在《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌中多有涉及的無數傳說都以這些故事為通識，其中雙馬神被描繪為恩人或拯救者。
他們所拯救的人物可在某處¹ Kv. I, 117, 9, puru varpamsi Asvina dadhana. ¹ Kv. I, 117, 9, puru varpamsi Asvina dadhana.
2 神譜（Theogony），第211-232行。

在某些情況下，他們曾是真實的人物，而關於他們我們當然無法得知任何事情。但在其他情況下，那些被他們拯救者顯然與拯救者本身一樣，都是神話人物¹。這些故事極為相似，這本身就表明了它們的神話性質。這些故事敘述他們所偏愛者遭遇的不幸有很大的相似性，以及他們被阿濕溫神（Asvins）拯救的方式也是如此。這些故事本身往往興味索然，以至於除非它們奠基於神話基礎之上，否則幾乎不會被記錄下來。因此我們讀到，一隻鵪鶉，瓦爾蒂卡（Vartika），曾被狼吞下，但卻被阿濕溫神從狼的嘴裡拯救了出來。即使我們將瓦爾蒂卡視為一位真實婦女的名字，她從狼的嘴裡被拯救這件事也幾乎不足以激發各位吠陀詩人去記述，而且也不清楚阿濕溫神如何能救她脫離被吞噬的命運，除非我們與艾提哈斯卡人（Aitihasikas）一樣，將他們視為兩位古代國王。
瓦爾帝迦（Vartika）
Vartika 在梵文中意為鶉鳥，對應於希臘文 ὄρνις。其詞源義為「歸返之鳥」，如同 Vertumnus（歸返或迴圈的年份）。那麼，若阿斯溫人（Asvins）曾經從狼的血盆大口中拯救了一隻真實的鶉鳥或一位真實的女性，¹ ¹ 見 Myriantheus, *Die Acvins oder Arischen Dioskuren*, ²《語言科學》第二卷，第 626 頁。
在古希臘文中，xeλιδών（燕子）象徵著年歲的迴圈，見 Bergk, p. 1034，η δὲ χελιδών, καλὰς ὥρας ἄγουσα, καλοὺς ἐνιαυτούς。

維爾蒂卡。585 這樣的事件豈不應該被銘刻在吠陀神話中嗎？當然，我們必須在別處尋求對我們的維爾蒂卡神話的解釋。維爾蒂卡，即「回歸者」，可以很好地代表白晝或春季的回歸。如果她代表白晝的回歸，即黎明的回歸，她確實是被阿斯溫二神（Asvins）從黑夜的狂獗之中拯救出來的；如果她代表年份的回歸，她可能是從冬季監禁中解救出來的春季。還應記住，赫拉克勒斯，明顯是太陽英雄，曾一度被一隻鵪鶉（Ortyx）所復甦（Gerhard, Griech. Myth., § 355, 2）。我們已經看到這兩個觀念——晨曦的和春季的——在古代神話中是如何不斷交織在一起的¹。那麼，如果我們發現勒托在鵪鶉島上生下她的兩個孩子阿波羅和阿爾特彌斯，我們幾乎無法將此視為純粹巧合，反而必須認識到這裡同樣是對於古老故事的輕微改變的重複：太陽神在晨曦歸來或春季初始時被生下。這樣我們就能理解為什麼阿波羅被稱為「狼殺者」（Lykoktonos），為什麼他的妹妹阿爾特彌斯被稱為鵪鶉島的守護者（Ortygia）。那個島嶼，鵪鶉島，最初是神話的預設而非地理現實，最為人所知的名字是德洛斯，這個名字意味著光明的島嶼或晨曦島嶼，也就是東方的天空。勒托生育 ¹ Sonne, K. Z., x, p. 121. 在德國，鸛鳥被視為「光明的鳥」（λυγ'ός ἱαρός），有一首現代警句詩出自奧勒阿里烏斯：「Ver laetum rediit, rediitque ciconia grata」（春天歡樂而至，感謝的鸛鳥歸來）。cf. Grimm, Mythologie, p. 723.

阿波羅與阿爾特彌斯的誕生後來經由詩人與說書者的各種闡發而得到發展，但該傳說的原初要素不必多於數句諺語，例如「狼吞噬了鶉」，即夜晚；「鶉從狼的口中被拯救出來」，即清晨；「夜晚生育了她的雙胞胎」，即新的一日（晝與夜）已開始。在尚未分化的雅利安神話狀態中，我們只有理由期待這樣的民間諺語；儘管如此，若梵文中的 *vartika* 與希臘文中的 ὄρτυξ（= *vartuka*）這兩個詞的出現證明了鶉在雅利安人分散之前就為其所知，那麼 *Vartika* 被馬神（Asvins）從狼的口中拯救出來，以及阿爾特彌斯（Artemis）與獵狼者阿波羅（Apollon Lykoktonos）在鶉島（Ortygia）誕生這些事實，則證明了那些備受貶低的太陽神話的最初萌芽，已在希臘人與印度人的祖先仍然緊密接觸時期開始萌生。誠然，說鶉可能曾是一種圖騰或物神是更為容易的，也完全可能雅利安人從不食用鶉，在此情況下，我們就應該被告知無須深究或為可疑的語源學所困擾。

我反而認為毗笈多（Vartika）的故事應當成為我們的教訓，我們應當學習一部上古神話在傳入我們手中之前，曾被打碎成多少片段。乍看之下，鶉鳥從狼的血盆大口中被拯救的故事，似乎與阿波羅與阿耳特彌斯在得洛斯島出生的故事沒有任何共同之處。然而，當我們看到阿耳特彌斯被稱為歐爾圖吉亞（Ortygia），同樣的名字也被賦予得洛斯島，而在吠陀中從狼口中被解救的人物被稱為毗笈多（Vartika）時，我們開始感覺到必定有某種理由存在。若我們承認在最遠古的雅利安時代，毗笈多（Vartika）既表示鶉鳥，又表示曙光，我們就能理解那個上古故事——永遠迴返的曙光從夜晚的黑暗或狼的血盆大口中被解救——如何在數世紀、甚至在這些上古傳說的最初起源與吠陀諸位聖仙或希臘吟遊詩人的時代相隔的數千年光陰中，呈現出這些迥然不同的面貌。無論如何，我們可以學到一個教訓：如果在此案例中由阿斯溫神所從狼口中解救的生命是曙光與初升的太陽，那麼其他由阿斯溫神所解救的生命也可能具有相似的起源；而如果毗笈多（Vartika）代表迴返的光明，那麼被殺狼者（Lykoktonos）所殺死的狼，幾乎不可能被意指為退卻的黑暗之外的任何東西。
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」是：iTyavtlna 這似乎不是有效的英文文本，可能是： 1. 打字錯誤或亂碼 2. 編碼問題導致的亂碼 3. 不完整的文本片段 請您重新提供正確的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行專業翻譯。

我們在吠陀讚歌中讀到，雙馬神（Asvins）將齊亞瓦納（Zyavana）恢復年輕，解救他脫離衰老的軀體，使他再次得到妻子的接納。我們可以在此例中看出這些古老故事是如何早已被擴充和多樣化的，因為雖然讚歌中的記述僅止於此，但在《百路梵書》（Satapatha-Brahmawa）IV, 1,5 中我們讀到了更為完整的敘述。我們被告知齊亞瓦納的妻子名叫蘇甘雅（Sukanya，意為「好女孩」），而她被賜予給年邁衰敗的仙人吉亞瓦納（Jiyavana）。後來的形式是迦亞瓦納（Kyavana）而非吉亞瓦納（ifyavana）。

由國王薩里亞塔（*Saryata*）所賜，以補償國王人民向她所施加的侮辱。之後阿斯溫雙神（Asvins）前來試圖引誘她離開，但她不願拋棄年邁的丈夫，甚至嘲笑了阿斯溫雙神。當阿斯溫雙神想知道為何被她嘲笑時，她承諾如果他們願意讓她丈夫恢復年輕就會告訴他們。於是阿斯溫雙神讓再耶婆那（Zyavana）踏入一池水，承諾他可以以任何他所希望的年齡從中出來。事成之後，再耶婆那告訴阿斯溫雙神，他們被嘲笑是因為他們曾被眾神排除於一場莊嚴的祭祀之外。這個故事可能在讚歌成立時已經存在，因為它包含在其他雅利安神話中也為人知的某些神話元素。在冥王哈得斯（Hades）之地開放以接納他與波瑟芬妮（Persephone），以及赫拉克勒斯（Herakles）據說創立盛大年祭的地點附近，有一股泉水，名為基阿涅（Kyane）。這池名為薩依薩婆（saisava），意為「青春」，似乎對應德語的容克布倫寧（Jungbrunnen，古高地德語 quickprunno，參見葛林《德國神話學》第554頁），而作為老翁之妻的蘇迦尼亞（Sukanya），將使許多人想起厄俄斯與堤托諾斯的故事。至於再耶婆那的名字，後作亥耶婆那（Jfyavana），係衍生自詞根 kyu，而 &yu 已被認定為對應 crevco，表達任何劇烈的運動，不僅是驅動或推動的動作，也包括下落，如梵文「tara ambaraA: Ayavante」意為「星辰自天空墜落或沒沒」（《羅摩衍那》卷五，13節，31行），或「svargaA; Ayavate lokat」，意為「他從須彌樂界墜落」。
**YAVANA。589** 世界。』因此它可能被用來指日落或下沉的太陽，對這樣的太陽確實可以說它已墜入西方熾熱或黑暗的深淵——正是那個提婆雙子神（Asvins）誕生之處，見《梨俱吠陀》卷三第39頌第3節，*tapusha budhne a ita*——而它在清晨又由提婆雙子神重新賦予生命而升起。提婆雙子神如脫去舊衣裳般移除雅瓦那身體的觀念，也可在圍繞太陽並於日落時隱沒它的雲彩中找到解釋¹。
阿特里（Atri）。

關於阿特里（Atri）有著大致相同的故事。據說他曾被投入一個火坑，有時被稱為 ribisa²，一個爐灶，這似乎是另一個比喻，用以表示熊熊燃燒的夕陽。阿斯溫神保護他免受高熱和黑暗之苦，並恢復了他的青春和力量。在這個案例中，阿特里的名字不如耶耶瓦那（iiyavana）那般清晰，然而它可能與阿維斯陀語的 atar 相關，其痕跡保存在拉丁文的 atrium 以及梵文的 athar-van 中（意為火祭司）。另有人將阿特里解釋為 ad-tH，意為「吞噬者」，這是眾神之敵的常見名稱（古北歐語 jotunn）³。在這類情況下，我們所能做的不過是提出建議；我¹發現了相同的解釋見於 V. 亨利（V. Henry）的《自然神話》（Mythes Naturalistes），第 12 頁。同一位學者也將西西弗斯（Sisyphus）的名字從根詞 ftyu 推導而出，其意為推動，我認為他是正確的。
